claims.claim: Battery spontaneous internal heat release begins from decomposition of SEI film at 80–120 °C.
claims.quote: The battery starts spontaneous internal heat release from the decomposition of SEI film at 80–120 ℃ (Spotnitz and Franklin, 2003).

claims.claim: The final temperature of graphite anode decomposition with electrolyte accumulates to higher than 250 °C.
claims.quote: The final temperature of graphite anode decomposition with electrolyte accumulates to higher than 250 C (Feng et al., 2018).

claims.claim: For a fully charged 18650 LIB, the highest thermal runaway temperature reached 727.64 °C.
claims.quote: Considering a 18650 LIB with full charge, the highest thermal runaway temperature reached 727.64 ℃ in the work of Zhao et al. (2020).

claims.claim: Under identical heating conditions, a battery at 25% SOC took 1.2 times longer to reach thermal runaway than a battery at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Zhao et al. (2020) suggested that under identical heating conditions, the battery with 25% SOC reached thermal runaway using 1.2 times longer than the battery with 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway time of a new LIB under the same conditions was 1.6 times longer than that of a LIB exposed to 1000 charge-discharge cycles.
claims.quote: Jung et al. (2022) estimated that the thermal runaway time of a new LIB under the same conditions was 1.6 times longer than that of the LIB that was exposed to 1000 charge-discharge cycles.

claims.claim: Increasing space confinement degrees shortens the TRP time interval by enhancing convection from ejected gases and radiation from flame.
claims.quote: Simulation results demonstrate that increasing the space confinement degrees shortens the propagation time interval by enhancing the convection from ejected gases and the radiation from flame.

claims.claim: Reduction of ceiling height extends the flame extension length and significantly accelerates cell-to-cell failure propagation.
claims.quote: The reduction of ceiling height extends the flame extension length and significantly accelerates the cell-to-cell failure, highlighting the impacts of jet fire on TR propagation.

claims.claim: Under open-air conditions, the cell dissipates heat through the upper surface, which decelerates TRP, whereas under ceiling conditions, thermal convection of ejected gases and radiation of jet fire above the batteries promote propagation.
claims.quote: The released energy is dissipated through the cell upper surface in the open-air conditions, which decelerates TRP. On the contrary, the thermal convection of ejected gases and radiation of jet fire above the batteries promote the propagation in existence of ceiling.

claims.claim: For the top ceiling condition, TRP time intervals Δt1-2 and Δt2-3 were reduced to 111 s and 103 s respectively, compared to 495 s and 303 s under open-air conditions.
claims.quote: the TRP time interval between the cell 1 and cell 2, marked as Δt1(cid:0) 2, was 495 s and the time interval between cell 2 and cell 3 Δt2(cid:0) 3 was 303 s. For the top ceiling conditions, Δt1(cid:0) 2 and Δt2(cid:0) 3 were reduced to 111 s and 103 s, respectively.

claims.claim: As ceiling height increased from 5 cm to 10 cm, the average TRP time interval between adjacent batteries increased by 203.8%, and raising the ceiling height to 15 cm further weakened propagation behavior with an average TRP time interval of approximately 389 s.
claims.quote: When the plate heights increased to 10 cm, the onset of TR for the cell 1 was delayed by 49 s and the average TRP time interval between adjacent batteries was found to increase by 203.8%. As the plate height continued to rise by 5 cm, it could be found that the propagation behaviour of TR has been effectively weakened, and the average TRP time interval was approximately 389 s.

claims.claim: An increase in road slope of 5% results in an increase in battery temperature of 3% and a reduction in battery SOC of 20%.
claims.quote: an increase in the road slope of 5 % results in an increase in the battery temperature of 3 % and a reduction in the SOC of the battery for 20 %

claims.claim: For a travel path with an average road slope of 5 degrees, the battery temperature increases by approximately 7°C, which requires more thermal management using hybrid cooling.
claims.quote: for a travel path with an average road slope of 5◦, the battery temperature increases about 7 0C which requires more thermal management using hybrid cooling

claims.claim: A higher state of charge (SOC) corresponds to a more pronounced progression of thermal runaway, a more impressive peak heat radiation, a more intense thermal runaway strength, and a greater quantity of smoke during the runaway phase.
claims.quote: a higher state of charge (SOC) corresponds to a more pronounced progression of thermal runaway, a more impressive peak heat radiation, a more intense thermal runaway strength, and a greater quantity of smoke during the runaway phase of a cell

claims.claim: In the Munich driving cycle, battery temperature rose by nearly 8 degrees in 6000 seconds under an assumed ambient temperature of 22 degrees Celsius.
claims.quote: the temperature has risen by nearly 8 degrees in 6000 seconds. As a result, the environment in this experiment is assumed to be 22 degrees

claims.claim: The WLTC cycle generates more heat at its maximum than previous cycles (NEDC and FTP-75), which can be attributed to more travel and a faster average speed.
claims.quote: this cycle generates more heat at its maximum than previous cycles, which can be due to more travel and, consequently, a faster average speed than in other cycles

claims.claim: The stress-based early warning method identifies the transition point from exponential to linear growth in surface stress, enabling thermal runaway prediction approximately 2000 s in advance for 50% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: By monitoring transition point of stress variation, thermal runaway can be predicted up to approximately 2000 s in advance.

claims.claim: For 0% SOC batteries that do not undergo thermal runaway, the stress signal provides a warning approximately 816.3 s before the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: Even in cases where 0 % SOC battery does not undergo thermal runaway, the stress signal can provide a warning approximately 816.3 s before the safety valve opens, significantly enhancing evacuation and rescue time.

claims.claim: A stress change rate exceeding 0.45 kPa/s is defined as the threshold value indicating the starting point of the early warning for thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the stress change rates corresponding to the transition points of these two stages are both higher than 0.47 kPa/s. Therefore, considering the safety margin, 0.45 kPa/s can be regarded as the starting value for the warning.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway reaches 427.3°C for the 100% SOC battery, compared to 317.1°C for the 50% SOC battery, a difference of approximately 110°C.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway, the maximum temperature (TTR, max) reaches 427.3 ℃ for the 100 % SOC battery, compared to only 317.1 ℃ for the 50 % SOC battery. Notably, the maximum temperature for the 100 % SOC battery exceeds that for the 50 % SOC battery by nearly 110 ℃.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger time for the 100% SOC battery occurs nearly 1200 s earlier than for the 50% SOC battery under the same overheating conditions.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway trigger time for the 100 % SOC is nearly 1200 s earlier than that for the 50 % SOC.

claims.claim: The peak mass loss rate during thermal runaway for the 100% SOC battery is 7.24 g/s, significantly higher than the 2.72 g/s observed for the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate curve for the 50 % SOC battery peaks at 2.72 g/s during thermal runaway, significantly lower than the peak value of 7.24 g/s observed for the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: The second peak stress during thermal runaway for the 50% SOC battery is 138.25 kPa, lower than the 224.88 kPa observed for the 100% SOC battery, attributed to slower chemical reactions at lower SOC.
claims.quote: the second peak stress for the 50 % SOC battery is 138.25 kPa, lower than the 224.88 kPa observed in the 100 % SOC battery. This difference is attributed to the slower chemical reaction in the 50 % SOC battery during thermal runaway compared to the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: For 280 Ah LFP batteries, thermal runaway does not occur at 0% SOC even when the front surface temperature approaches 400°C, because most lithium ions have been transferred to the positive electrode, resulting in slower chemical reactions.
claims.quote: for the 0 % SOC battery, the temperature rise rate remains slow until Tf approaches 400 ℃, suggesting that thermal runaway does not occur... when the battery's SOC is at 0 %, most of the lithium ions in the negative electrode have been transferred to the positive electrode, leading to a slower chemical reaction rate and a gradual voltage drop, which prevents thermal runaway from occurring.

claims.claim: When the safety valve opens on 280 Ah LFP batteries, the battery temperature decreases to some extent due to the Joule-Thomson effect, as high-pressure gas expands through the valve and performs work on the external environment.
claims.quote: the temperature of the batteries at different SOCs decreases to some extent when the safety valve opens, which is attributed to the Joule-Thomson effect... As the gas expands through the valve to perform work on the external environment, the overall temperature decreases, leading to a reduction in the battery's temperature.

claims.claim: Traditional monitoring methods such as voltage drop and temperature surge provide warning only within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, whereas gas detection via safety valve opening provides warning nearly 1000 s in advance.
claims.quote: traditional methods, such as monitoring voltage drop or temperature surge, are only effective within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, posing a high risk. Detection of gas release can provide an early warning of thermal runaway nearly 1000 s in advance.

claims.claim: The reaction onset temperature for cathode-anode and anode-electrolyte reactions roughly advances (decreases) as SOC increases in NCM523/graphite 18650 batteries.
claims.quote: the reaction onset temperature for cathode-anode and anode-electrolyte roughly advances as the SOC increases

claims.claim: The reaction enthalpy of cathode-anode, anode-electrolyte, and cathode reactions increases with increasing SOC in NCM523/graphite 18650 batteries.
claims.quote: the reaction enthalpy of the cathode-anode, anode-electrolyte and cathode increases with the increase of SOC

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, anode-electrolyte and cathode-anode reactions account for 52.2% and 40.9% of total heat generation during thermal runaway, respectively, while cathode and separator account for only 5.6% and 1.2%.
claims.quote: the heat production from the anode-electrolyte sample and the anode-cathode sample are the main heat of the battery during TR, accounting for 52.2% and 40.9% respectively at 100% SOC, while heat productions from cathode and separator only account for 5.6% and 1.2% respectively

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the maximum temperature rise rate of the NCM523/graphite 18650 battery is 12.564 °C/min, which does not meet the thermal runaway criterion, and therefore thermal runaway does not occur.
claims.quote: at 0% SOC, the maximum temperature rise rate of the battery is 12.564 °C/min, which does not meet the criteria for TR, and therefore there is no T5 for battery with 0% SOC

claims.claim: The battery safety valve opening temperature decreases as SOC increases, indicating that the rate of gas production within the battery increases with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: the battery safety valve opening temperature decreases as the SOC increases, indicating that the rate of gas production within the battery increases as the SOC increases

claims.claim: The abnormal expansion force can be detected at temperatures as low as 35.4°C during overcharging, which achieves an early warning signal 11 minutes earlier than the onset of battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The result shows that the abnormal expansion force can be detected at temperatures as low as 35.4 ◦C, which achieves an early warning signal 11 min earlier than the onset of battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The time interval between the warning signal of the expansion force and temperature increases steadily from 151 s to 682 s as the charging rate decreases from 6 C to 1 C.
claims.quote: the time interval between the warning signal of the expansion force and temperature increases steadily from 151 s to 682 s as the charging rate decreases.

claims.claim: The charging rate hardly affects the SOC boundary of venting, which remains at approximately ±118% SOC regardless of charging rate.
claims.quote: the charging rate hardly affects the stage of charge boundary of venting, which is around ±118 %.

claims.claim: At charging rates of 5 C and 6 C, the internal short-circuit temperature (TSC) decreased significantly to 101.2°C, compared to approximately 120°C at charging rates of 1–4 C, attributed to rapid proliferation of Li dendrites at high charging rates.
claims.quote: Based on the temperature at which the voltage drop rate reached the maximum (TSC), the TSC at CRs of 1–4 C was approximately 120 ◦C. However, at CRs of 5 C and 6 C, TSC decreased significantly and the SC temperature decreased to 101.2 ◦C. This phenomenon is attributable to the rapid proliferation of Li dendrites within batteries subjected to high CRs.

claims.claim: The maximum expansion-force rise rate ((dF/dt)Max) before safety valve venting increased from 31.4 N/s at 1 C to 505.6 N/s at 6 C, indicating a strong positive correlation between charging rate and maximum force rise rate.
claims.quote: (dF/dt)Max increased continuously with the CR. In particular, the effect of the CR on (dF/dt)Max was extremely significant, with (dF/dt)Max reaching 31.4 N/s and 505.6 N/s at CRs of 1 C and 6 C, respectively.

claims.claim: When the charging rate was lower than 3 C, a plateau stage was observed in the expansion-force rise rate during battery overcharging, but the plateau disappeared at charging rates higher than 3 C.
claims.quote: When the charging rate was lower than 3 C, there would be a plateau stage was observed for the expansion-force rise rate during battery overcharging, while the plateau disappeared with charge rate higher than 3 C, and the expansion-force rise rate continued to increase.

claims.claim: The SOC boundary at which the battery is completely filled with gas decreases as the charging rate increases during overcharging, specifically from 105% SOC at 1 C to 103% SOC at 6 C.
claims.quote: the SOC boundary at which the battery is completely filled with gas decreases as the CR increases during overcharging.

claims.claim: The efficacy of the expansion-force early warning method at the module and system levels requires further additional validation and cannot be directly extrapolated from cell-level results.
claims.quote: its efficacy at the module and system levels requires further additional validation. For instance, in configurations involving multiple rows of modules, it becomes necessary to equip each row with a force sensor.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition becomes significant above 60°C and can be described by an Arrhenius equation.
claims.quote: The decomposition of SEI becomes significant at elevated temperatures. According to reference [36], it becomes significant above 60 C and can be described by Arrhenius equation

claims.claim: At elevated temperatures, SEI decomposition overrides SEI formation, reducing SEI layer thickness until parts of the graphite anode are exposed to the electrolyte, leading to exothermic SEI regeneration reactions.
claims.quote: At elevated temperatures, the SEI decomposition reaction overrides SEI formation [6]. The end result of such a regime is the reduction of the SEI layer thickness to an extent that parts of graphite anode are exposed to the electrolyte. Direct contact between electrolyte and lithiated graphite leads to the reaction of SEI regeneration [6], i.e. reactions between exposed intercalated Li in the anode with the surrounding electrolyte.

claims.claim: At low currents (C/20 rate), the AET model with phase-separating LFP material shows significantly higher heat generation over a wide range of intermediate depths of discharge compared to the Newman secondary particles approach, due to inter-particle Li redistribution from particle-by-particle lithiation.
claims.quote: Fig. 5b shows that at low currents heat generation is significantly higher in wide range of intermediate DoDs, within spinodal regions of the chemical potential [26], for the AET case. This is, as presented in Fig. 6b, reasoned by the inter-particle Li redistribution, because of the so-called particle-by-particle lithiation of phase separated particles at lower local overpotentials and thus at low currents

claims.claim: Higher transport-driven heat generation in the AET cathode model initiates the chain of phenomena leading to thermal runaway much earlier compared to the Newman secondary particles model under adiabatic low-rate cycling.
claims.quote: it is obvious that higher transport driven heat generation of the AET case initiates the chain of phenomena resulting in the outbreak of the thermal runaway much earlier compared to the model relying on Newman secondary particles.

claims.claim: SEI regeneration causes significant loss of cyclable lithium, resulting in capacity loss, lower cell voltage, higher overpotentials, increased transport losses, and a further increase in heat generation rate.
claims.quote: SEI regeneration is namely associated with significant loss of cyclable Li thus resulting in loss of cell capacity and lower cell voltage. Lower cell voltage namely originates from the higher overpotentials caused by additional passivating layers and increased transport losses both further promoting the rate of heat generation.

claims.claim: TRF propagation speed accelerates inside each battery cell, ranging from 3.6 to 10.6 mm/s during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: TRF propagation speed is found to be accelerated inside each cell (from 3.6 to 10.6 mm/s) during TRP, driven by a significant temperature gradient, chemical reactions, and gas flow along the TRP direction.

claims.claim: The in-cell TRF acceleration behavior is more noticeable for batteries with more jelly rolls.
claims.quote: The in-cell TRF acceleration behavior is more noticeable for batteries with more jelly rolls.

claims.claim: The limit temperature value of each cell front temperature during TRF propagation is much higher than the TR triggering temperature, meaning the TRP process cannot be eliminated under tested conditions.
claims.quote: The limit temperature value of each cell front temperature is much higher than the TR triggering temperature. Therefore, the TRP process cannot be eliminated. TR of the next jelly roll/cell will be triggered, which is also proved by the experiments.

claims.claim: Decreasing the constant C in the TRF boundary temperature equation, which can be achieved by lower thermal conductivity, higher heat capacity, thicker thermal resistance layer, higher convection coefficients, or heat absorption components, leads to TRF propagation suppression.
claims.quote: Considering β(γ+1+βγ)2/(3+β)(1−2γ) < 0, decreasing the constant C can be achieved by the intrinsic method: lower thermal conductivity and higher heat capacity; supplementary strategies: thicker thermal resistance layer, higher convection coefficients, or heat absorption components.

claims.claim: The venting direction of particle/fire changes during TRF propagation in multi-jelly roll prismatic batteries, pointing to lines between each triggered jelly roll and the safety valve, with one and three noticeable venting direction changes observed for Sample A and Sample B respectively.
claims.quote: The particle/fire venting direction changes during the TRF propagation in multi-jelly roll prismatic batteries, which points to the lines between each triggered jelly roll and the safety valve. There are one and three noticeable venting direction changes for the TR tests of Sample A and Sample B, respectively.

claims.claim: T1 signifies the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to SEI decomposition, T2 represents the temperature at which thermal runaway is triggered often associated with separator failure leading to internal short circuit, and T3 designates the peak temperature of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: T1 signifies the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition. T2 represents the temperature at which TR is triggered, often associated with separator failure leading to an internal short circuit (ISC). At T2, the temperature transitions from a stable level to a quasi-exponential increase. T3 designates the peak temperature of TR and is identifiable as the highest point on the temperature profile.

claims.claim: Beyond 80°C, breakdown of the SEI layer becomes evident, and as temperature approaches 90°C, SEI decomposition initiates liberation of significant gases and heat including O2, CO2, and CH4.
claims.quote: Beyond 80 ◦C, the breakdown of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer becomes evident. As the temperature approaches 90 ◦C, the commencement of SEI decomposition initiates the liberation of significant gases and heat (O2, CO2 and, CH4)

claims.claim: At approximately 120°C, SEI decomposition on the anode surface exposes active material, causing lithium within the anode to react with the electrolyte and generating hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: Upon reaching approximately 120 ◦C, the SEI decomposition on the anode surface exposes the active material, causing the previously safeguarded lithium within the anode to react with the electrolyte, generating some hydrocarbons

claims.claim: Polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) based conventional polymer separators melt at around 130°C and 170°C respectively, and their thermal degradation leads to internal short circuit while liberating substantial energy that rapidly elevates battery temperature.
claims.quote: typically causing the polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) based conventional polymer separators to melt at around 130 ◦C and 170 ◦C respectively. The thermal degradation of the separator further leads to the battery's internal short circuit, while simultaneously liberating a substantial amount of energy that rapidly elevates the battery's temperature.

claims.claim: The decomposition of electrolyte salt LiPF6 accelerates significantly when temperatures surpass 150°C, and the resulting PF5 expedites electrolyte decomposition and reacts with H2O to emit toxic HF gas.
claims.quote: The decomposition of electrolyte salts, (for example LiPF6) experiences significant acceleration when temperatures surpass 150 ◦C. The resulting PF5 expedites electrolyte decomposition and reacts with H2O to emit toxic HF gas

claims.claim: In the air interstice configuration, radiation accounts for more than 80% of the total heat flux on the exposed surface of the closest neighboring cell (cell 4).
claims.quote: it is observed that the radiative effect accounts for the majority of the total heat flux on the exposed surface of cell 4 (above 80%), which is coherent with the literature

claims.claim: For the air interstice case with standard emissivity (ε = 0.8), the closest neighbor cell (cell 4) reached a peak temperature of 136 °C at 490 s, which was the highest peak temperature among all four separator configurations tested.
claims.quote: the air interstice case with regular emissivity (ε = 0.8) is the one with the highest peak temperature (136 ◦C at 490 s), followed by ceramic paper (132 ◦C at 900 s), G7 (129 ◦C at 150 s) and air interstice with reduced emissivity ε = 0.2 (124 ◦C at 700 s)

claims.claim: The ceramic paper fiber separator configuration is 23% lighter than the G7 separator configuration in the half-module assembly.
claims.quote: the ceramic paper fiber configuration is also the one with the largest energy density, 253.4 Wh/kg... the configuration with ceramic paper fiber is also 23% lighter than the one with G7

claims.claim: When four cells were simultaneously triggered by ISC, thermal runaway propagated to neighboring cells 4, 5, 6, 8, and 9, with cells 4 and 6 reaching a temperature spike at 224 s and cell 5 at 252 s.
claims.quote: a cascading effect has been observed, with cells 4, 6, 5, 8, and 9 reaching TR due to thermal abuse, from the energy released by the 4 ISC-triggered cells. From Fig. 31, it is noticed that cells 4 and 6 present very similar thermal behaviors, with temperature curves overlapping and reaching a spike at 224 s. Cell 5 is also subjected to a temperature spike, however, it occurs at 252 s.

claims.claim: In the single ISC-triggered cell scenarios, a thermal runaway temperature spike was not observed in any neighboring cell for any of the four separator configurations tested, because the cathode–electrolyte and electrolyte decomposition reactions were not sufficiently activated.
claims.quote: heat transferred from the ISC-triggered cell does provoke an initial effect of internal energy release from exothermic reactions... However, as seen in Section 5.2, for the temperature increase effect that leads to TR, the cell must be driven to trigger cathode + electrolyte reaction and electrolyte decomposition... Reaction transition did not occur, as per Fig. 27, and energy release was not enough to produce an intense internally induced temperature surge.

claims.claim: When the equivalent heat transfer coefficient h is greater than 12.5 W/(m2 K), no thermal runaway is observed in the simulations, because the rate of heat dissipation from the battery exceeds the internal heat release rate.
claims.quote: No TR is observed when h is greater than 12.5 W/(m2 K) in our simulations. This is justifiable because under higher h condition, when the LIB temperature exceeds that of the ambient, the rate of heat dissipation from the battery increases and exceeds the HRR inside the LIB.

claims.claim: At h = 2.5 W/(m2 K), the maximum temperature rise rate reaches 291.6 K/min and the maximum temperature is 603 K, which is 41 K above the maximum temperature of the baseline case (h = 5.5 W/(m2 K)).
claims.quote: the maximum TRR reaches 291.6 K/min and the maximum temperature is 603 K, 41 K above the maximum temperature of the baseline.

claims.claim: The PE-E reaction is the main cause of the battery reaching the critical thermal runaway temperature rise rate (RTR) in a short time, specifically under low heat transfer coefficient conditions (h < 7.5 W/(m2 K)) leading to TR.
claims.quote: at low h conditions (<7.5 W/(m2 K)) leading LIB TR, the PE-E reaction is the main factor that leads the LIB reaching the critical TR temperature increasing rate.

claims.claim: Reducing the negative electrode active material volume fraction fNE by 5.0 % significantly reduces the thermal runaway risk in terms of temperature and time.
claims.quote: Reducing the fNE(cid:0) E by 5.0 % will significantly reduce the TR risk in terms of the temperature and time.

claims.claim: With the same percentage reduction applied to fPE and fNE, reducing the volume fraction of the negative electrode material more effectively delays the battery from reaching maximum temperature, providing more time for thermal management.
claims.quote: reducing the volume fraction of the negative electrode material more effectively delays the battery from reaching Tmax, providing more time for thermal management.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway occurs when the Thermal Runaway Number (TRN) exceeds 1, where TRN is defined by the condition 2βR²/(kμ₁²) - 1 = 0 for a cylindrical cell.
claims.quote: it has been shown that thermal runaway occurs when the value of TRN exceeds 1. Mathematically, for a cylindrical cell, this threshold condition is given by 2βR²/(kμ₁²) - 1 = 0

claims.claim: For a low thermal conductivity cell (0.15 W/m K), Tcritical rises with increasing convective heat transfer coefficient h but becomes stable beyond h = 150 W/m² K, indicating that increasing external convective cooling beyond this limit does not delay thermal runaway.
claims.quote: for low thermal conductivity of 0.15 W/m K, Tcritical rises with increase in h, but becomes stable beyond h = 150 W/m2 K. However, for a high thermal conductivity cell, Tcritical continues to increase even for higher h values.

claims.claim: For a cell with thermal conductivity of 1.0 W/m K, the oven temperature must exceed 175 °C to cause thermal runaway, compared to 160 °C for the lowest thermal conductivity considered.
claims.quote: For a cell with thermal conductivity of 1.0 W/m K, for example, the oven temperature needs to be above 175 °C in order to cause thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Tcritical increases as carbon content at the anode (Wc) decreases, because larger Wc leads to more aggressive heat generation, causing thermal runaway at lower temperatures.
claims.quote: Tcritical increases as Wc goes down. This occurs because the larger the value of Wc, the more aggressive heat generation is, and therefore, thermal runaway occurs at lower temperatures.

claims.claim: There exists a lower limit of cell thermal conductivity k below which a Tcritical of 145 °C or above is not achievable even with extremely aggressive thermal management such as liquid cooling.
claims.quote: there is a lower limit of the value of k, below which a Tcritical of 145 °C or above is simply not possible, even with extremely aggressive thermal management, such as liquid cooling.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C with out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK and convective heat transfer coefficient of 5 W/m²K at 30°C ambient temperature, thermal runaway does not occur, but discharge rates higher than 15C lead to chain reactions and thermal runaway.
claims.quote: even a high discharge rate of up to 15C may not result in thermal runaway under the conditions considered in this analysis. The out-of-plane thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coeﬃcient considered are 0.25 W/mK and 5 W/m2 K, respectively... for discharge rates higher than 15C, the chain reactions consisting of diﬀerent decompositions occurs ultimately leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C, ambient temperatures below 30°C do not result in thermal runaway, whereas ambient temperatures of 40°C or higher result in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: ambient temperatures less than 30 °C don't result in thermal runaway which is consistent with the results from Fig. 6(a). However, ambient temperatures of 40 °C or higher result in thermal runaway as per the model prediction.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 15C and 30°C ambient temperature, only SEI decomposition takes place, producing insufficient heat to trigger subsequent decompositions; at 18C, the negative electrode–solvent reaction is additionally triggered, leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: For the discharge rate of 15C, only SEI decomposition takes place, which results in a very limited amount of heat generation. The total heat generation and temperature increase for this case is insuﬃcient to trigger subsequent decompositions and reactions. For the higher discharge rate of 18C, in addition to SEI decomposition, the reaction between the negative electrode and solvent is triggered. The total heat generation in this case causes temperature to increase enough to initiate a chain reaction ultimately leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At a cell thickness of 15 mm and out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK, increasing thermal conductivity from 0.25 W/mK to 0.35 W/mK prevents thermal runaway at 15C discharge rate under natural convection.
claims.quote: thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK results in thermal runaway which may be prevented with a slightly higher thermal conductivity of 0.35 W/mK.

claims.claim: For a 15 mm thick cell with out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK at 25C discharge rate and 60°C ambient, boiling heat transfer in minichannels does not prevent thermal runaway because increased thermal resistance causes core temperature to exceed the threshold for chain reactions, unlike the 10 mm thick cell where boiling prevents thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Unlike the results presented in Fig. 10, the cell experiences thermal runaway even when boiling in minichannel is considered as the cooling mechanism. The increased thermal resistance of the cell causes the core temperature of the cell to exceed the threshold value to trigger chain reactions.

claims.claim: The heat contribution of the anode-electrolyte reaction is up to 63.8% of the total heat generated during thermal runaway of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: the heat contribution of anode-electrolyte reaction is up to 63.8% of the total heat

claims.claim: Internal short circuit is responsible for a small proportion of total heat generation during thermal runaway, yet is still a crucial inducement of chain reactions.
claims.quote: internal short circuit is responsible for a little proportion of total heat generation during thermal runaway which is contrary to the general thoughts, yet still a crucial inducement of chain reactions

claims.claim: SEI decomposition starts at 69°C and the balanced reaction of SEI decomposition and regeneration does not stop until the temperature rises to 250°C.
claims.quote: SEI is meta-stable and will start decompose at 69 ℃. SEI decomposition is universally acknowledged as the first side reaction that occurs during the TR process. The balanced reaction of the SEI decomposition and regeneration will not stop until the temperature rises to 250 ℃

claims.claim: A larger convection coefficient reduces the maximum thermal runaway temperature and postpones the onset time of thermal runaway, while having weak connection with the onset temperature of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The convection coefficient has weak connection with onset temperature of TR, but has a strong relationship with the onset time of TR... The orange dot illustrates maximum TR temperature of the battery falls with the convection coefficient increases.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway triggered by local overheating, the temperature difference within the battery system can reach up to 316°C at 970 s, indicating remarkable temperature non-uniformity, compared to approximately 130°C in oven tests.
claims.quote: During TR period III, the temperature difference can up to 316 ℃ at 970 s, an indicator of remarkable temperature non-uniformity... batteries in oven tests have a uniform temperature field during TR, and the temperature difference is about 130 ℃

claims.claim: For 18650 LIBs, thermal runaway reaction severity increased with higher SOC, faster penetration speed, and penetration at the cell centre.
claims.quote: They found that TR reaction was more severe when the battery was penetrated at the centre with faster penetration speed and the severity also increased with the SOC.

claims.claim: Increasing contact resistance between the nail and the cell from 0.05 Ω to 0.5 Ω decreases the maximum cell surface temperature by approximately 100 °C.
claims.quote: The results show that the maximum cell surface decrease by approximately 100 C when the contact resistance increases from 0.05 to 0.5.

claims.claim: Increasing contact resistance from 0.05 Ω to 0.5 Ω decreases the predicted nail tip temperature from 1200 °C to 700 °C, due to reduced current flow and lower Joule heating.
claims.quote: The predicted temperature of the nail tip decreases from 1200 to 700 C as the contact resistance increases from 0.05 to 0.5. The current flows through the nail decreases with the increase of the contact resistance, leading to the decrease of the Joule heating and accordingly lower temperature at the nail site due to lower heat generation and higher dissipation because of higher contact resistance.

claims.claim: For a 21700 NMC cell with 4800 mAh capacity, thermal runaway was only triggered when the internal short circuit resistance (RISC) was below 0.005 Ω; further reduction to 0.001 Ω considerably accelerated the evolution to TR.
claims.quote: it is estimated that the threshold internal short circuit resistance for the current 21,700 LIB with 4800 mAh lies between 0.005 Ω and 0.006 Ω as TR was only triggered below the RISC = 0.005 Ω. It is obvious from Eq.(6) that further reducing the resistance to RISC = 0.001 Ω would accelerate the speed of evolution to TR.

claims.claim: The predicted cell surface temperature differs by up to 350 °C between natural convection and forced convection cooling, indicating that forced convective cooling has the potential to prevent TR propagation in cell groups.
claims.quote: The predicted surface temperature differs by as much as 350 C from natural to forced convection, indicating the potential of forced convective cooling to prevent TR propagation in cell groups/modules.

claims.claim: Under natural convection (h = 2 W/m²K), varying cell surface emissivity causes up to 50 °C difference in predicted cell surface temperature, but under forced convection (h = 71.7 W/m²K) the effect of emissivity is negligible.
claims.quote: It can be seen from Fig. 15(a) that the temperature differs by up to 50 C in the case of natural convection (h = 2 W/m2K) which reflects the dominance of emissivity. The effect of emissivity can be neglected in forced convection cooling (h = 71.7 W/m2K), as depicted in Fig. 15 (b).

claims.claim: The onset temperature of self-heating for the tested 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC was 132.45 °C, and the thermal runaway temperature determined by EV-ARC was 195.58 °C.
claims.quote: From 132.45 C onwards, the temperature rise accelerates irreversibly till the maximum cell surface temperature. This is treated as the onset temperature Tonset of the self-heating stage... TTR and Tmax are the thermal runaway temperature which was 195.58 C determined by the EV-ARC tests for the present cell

claims.claim: In radial nail penetration tests, the flame lasted approximately 4 seconds, whereas in axial nail penetration tests the flame lasted approximately 18 seconds.
claims.quote: The flame lasted approximately 4 s in the Type R tests and 18 s in the Type A tests.

claims.claim: Both the maximum cell surface temperature and the rate of temperature rise were higher in radial nail penetration (within 10 s) than in axial nail penetration (within 20 s) for 21700 cylindrical LIBs.
claims.quote: both the maximum cell surface temperature and the rising rate of the temperature were found to be higher in the radial (within 10 s) than the axial nail penetration case (within 20 s).

claims.claim: The heat generated inside the penetrated nail (Qnail) was 9.77 J for radial penetration (10.05 mm depth) and 14.66 J for axial penetration (15 mm depth), which is very small compared to the heat from internal cell reactions.
claims.quote: The calculated Qnail is 9.77 J in the in the penetrated nail area of 10.05 mm for the case of radial penetration and 14.66 J in the penetrated area of 15 mm in the axial case, respectively. Hence, they are neglected while comparing the heat of reactions within the cell due its own components.

claims.claim: The optimum operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries is from 20°C to 40°C, and the temperature difference should be kept within 5°C.
claims.quote: The optimum operating temperature range of LIBs is from 20℃ to 40℃ [3–5], and the temperature difference should be kept within 5 ℃ [6].

claims.claim: Too low temperature increases the internal resistance and polarization internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries, increases power and energy loss, and reduces discharge capacity; too high temperature increases the degradation rate, reducing performance and cycle life.
claims.quote: Too low temperature will increase the internal resistance and polarization internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries, increase power and energy loss, and reduce the discharge capacity of the battery; too high temperature will increase the degradation rate of the battery, thereby reducing the performance and cycle life [2].

claims.claim: Metal foam can extend the total convection heat transfer area by nearly 10-fold, thereby significantly reducing the maximum temperature of batteries.
claims.quote: metal foam can extend the total convection heat transfer area by nearly 10-fold, thereby significantly reducing the maximum temperature of batteries.

claims.claim: When cooling air flows through metal foam, flow resistance increases significantly at low mass flow rate, causing relatively poor temperature difference of batteries.
claims.quote: it is inevitable that the flow resistance across the metal foam will increase significantly, which will cause relatively poor temperature difference of batteries at a low mass flow rate

claims.claim: Along the cooling air flow direction, the downstream temperature of the battery module is significantly higher than its upstream temperature, because cooling air absorbs heat continuously and its cooling capacity declines.
claims.quote: Along the cooling air flow direction, the downstream temperature of the battery module is significantly higher than its upstream temperature. This is because the cooling air will continue to absorb heat in the process of heat dissipation, resulting in its own cooling capacity decline, which makes the temperature of the battery show a trend of increasing along the direction of cooling air flow.

claims.claim: When insulation layer thickness is increased to 0.7 mm, TRP is ceased in both in-line and brick module configurations.
claims.quote: When the insulting layer thickness was further increased to 0.7 mm, the TRP was ceased for in both types of modules.

claims.claim: At insulation thickness of 0.6 mm, TRP is inhibited in the brick module but still occurs in the in-line module with a TRP time interval of approximately 1096 s.
claims.quote: It could be observed that the chain propagation of TR has been effectively inhibited in the brick module while it still occurred in the in-line module, whereas accompanied by a longer TRP time interval of roughly 1096 s.

claims.claim: When insulation layer thickness exceeds 2 mm, TRP can be completely ceased even when local failure of insulation occurs.
claims.quote: As the thickness increased to 2 mm, the number of TR cells was found to not improve and propagation has been prevented.

claims.claim: Local failure of thermal insulation initially leads to small-scale TR within the local scope, which contributes to larger heat accumulation and raises the parameter threshold needed to prevent TRP.
claims.quote: The local failure of inhibition strategy will initially lead to the small-scale battery TR within local scope, contributing a larger heat accumulation and potentiality of TRP.

claims.claim: When air velocity increases from 1 m/s to 4 m/s with 0.5 mm insulation, chain propagation of TR is effectively inhibited in both in-line and brick battery pack configurations.
claims.quote: As the air velocity was further increased to 4 m⋅s−1, the chain propagation of TR has been effectively inhibited in both types of battery packs.

claims.claim: TR propagation to Bat 2 is prevented when the TR triggering temperature TTR,ARC is raised above 469 °C, which is the maximum temperature that the front edges of cells inside Bat 2 can reach.
claims.quote: suppose that TTR,ARC is higher than 469 °C, TR propagation will be prevented because T}2_f(t) and T}2_b(t) will never reach TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: Delaying TR propagation from Bat 1 to Bat 2 leads to a higher temperature in Bat 2, which shortens the TR propagation time from Bat 2 to Bat 3 (D2→3).
claims.quote: a delay in the TR propagation from Bat 1 to Bat 2 may lead to a higher temperature in Bat 2, which leads to a much faster TR propagation from Bat 2 to Bat 3.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when the heat dissipation coefficient hdis exceeds 70 W m⁻² K⁻¹, because max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} only reaches 249 °C, which is below TTR,ARC.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when hdis is larger than 70 W m−2 K−1, because the max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t) can only reach 249 °C < TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when a 1 mm thermal resistant layer with thermal conductivity less than 0.2 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹ is inserted between adjacent batteries, limiting max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} to 226 °C, which is below TTR,ARC.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when kD equals or less than 0.2 W m−2 K−1, because the max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} can only reach 226 °C < TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when DHe is reduced to 75% or less of its original value, as shown by the condition on max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)}.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when DHe equals 75% or less of its original value.

claims.claim: As the radius of the ISC increased from 0.25 mm to 1.25 mm, the total heat energy generated increased from 4.1 kJ to 85.4 kJ, and the average discharge current increased from 0.257 A to 5.626 A.
claims.quote: As the short circuit size increased, the total heat energy increased from 4.1 kJ (r = 0.25 mm) to 85.4 kJ (r = 1.25 mm), and the average current also increased from 0.257 A (r = 0.25 mm) to 5.626 A (r = 1.25 mm).

claims.claim: When the ISC radius was 0.35 mm or greater in a 22 Ah pouch LIB cell, the local temperature of the ISC zone exceeded the thermal runaway trigger temperature of 150°C.
claims.quote: When the size of the short circuit was larger than 0.35 mm, T cell,max was relatively small, but T SZ,max exceeded the trigger point of thermal abuse. These findings demonstrate that to prevent thermal incidents, there must not be a hole with radius larger than 0.35 mm in the separators used in a 22 Ah pouch LIB cell.

claims.claim: With ISC area fixed at 1 mm², increasing the short circuit perimeter from 4 mm to 32.125 mm increased total heat generation from 19.3 kJ to 28.3 kJ, but decreased the peak ISC zone temperature from 228.7°C to 108.2°C.
claims.quote: As the short circuit perimeter increased from 4 mm to 32.125 mm and the short circuit resistance decreased, the total heat generation increased from 19.3 kJ to 28.3 kJ, and the time-averaged current increased from 1.23 A to 1.81 A. Although the low and narrow short circuits led to large currents and heat generation, the peak temperatures of the ISC zone, Tmax, SZ, were rather low... When the short circuit perimeter was 4.0 mm, Tmax, SZ was approximately 228.7℃, while for the short circuit perimeter of 32.125 mm, Tmax, SZ was 108.2℃.

claims.claim: The ISC zone temperature was lowest (130°C) when the short circuit was located at the center of the LIB cell, while TSZ,max exceeded 160°C when the short was located on the side, indicating that ISC location near the cell edge increases the risk of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of the ISC zone, TSZ,max, was lowest (130℃) when the short was located at the center of the LIB cell, while TSZ,max was higher than 160℃ when the short was located on the side.

claims.claim: In the 22 Ah pouch cell with a 0.5 mm radius ISC, approximately 91% of total heat generation occurred in the small ISC zone, and approximately 80% of total heat was generated at the cathode due to its low electrical conductivity of 10 S/m.
claims.quote: Most of the heat, 91% of the total heat generation, was generated in the small area of the ISC zone because the resistance of the short was relatively large and the discharge current was small... The amount of heat generated at the cathode by the passage of the short circuit current was approximately 3.8 W, and it accounted for more than 80% of the total heat generation. This was because the electrical conductivity of the cathode was significantly low at 10 S/m, which was approximately 10% of that of the anode.

claims.claim: The activation energy of the electrolyte global Arrhenius equation plays the most important role in affecting the delay time for thermal runaway among the seven global Arrhenius equations for NCM811PC.
claims.quote: For the seven global Arrhenius equations, the activation energy of electrolyte global equation plays the most important role in affecting the delay time for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: After the frequency factor A of electrolyte is increased by 5%, the activation energy Ea of electrolyte is decreased by 5%, and the reaction enthalpy change ΔH of electrolyte is increased, the reaction front propagation during thermal runaway of an NCM811PC cell with nail penetration can be successfully captured.
claims.quote: After the frequency factor A of electrolyte is increased by 5 %, the activation energy Ea of electrolyte is decreased by 5 %, and the reaction enthalpy change ΔH of electrolyte is increased, the simulated battery thermal runaway process is shown in Fig. 7 where the unit of the temperature is K. Fig. 7 clearly shows that the reaction front propagation during the thermal runaway process can be successfully captured.

claims.claim: Using the original Arrhenius kinetics parameters obtained from DSC experiments, the simulation of an NCM811PC cell with nail penetration showed no reaction front propagation and no thermal runaway, whereas experiments under this condition do show thermal runaway.
claims.quote: From Fig. 5 (bottom right), it is seen that not only is there no reaction front propagation, but the temperature of the nail is also decreased due to no heat addition. Experiments under this condition do show thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Compared to NCM811PC, NCM622PC has a slower heat release rate and lower mean temperature during thermal runaway, and at the same time point (around 7 s) the reaction front location of NCM622PC is behind that of NCM811PC.
claims.quote: Compared to NCM811PC, NCM622PC has slower heat release rate and lower mean temperature. At the same time (around 7 s), the reaction front location of NCM622PC is behind that of NCM811PC.

claims.claim: In the simulated venting gases of an NCM811PC pouch cell with nail penetration, CO, CO2, and H2O have large amounts, C2H4 and O2 have medium amounts, while CH4, C2H6, and H2 have small amounts.
claims.quote: In the venting chemical species, CO, CO2, and H2O have large amounts, C2H4 and O2 have medium amounts, while CH4, C2H6, and H2 have small amounts.

claims.claim: The SEI layer decomposes first and generates heat at a temperature of approximately 90°C, initiating the subsequent chemical reaction of the negative electrode.
claims.quote: The SEI layer is decomposed first because it generates heat at temperature of approximately 90 °C and initiates the subsequent chemical reaction Rne of the negative electrode

claims.claim: The electrolyte starts to decompose at temperatures exceeding 200°C and releases the greatest amount of exothermic heat among all electrochemical components.
claims.quote: the remaining electrolyte Re starts to decompose at temperatures exceeding 200 °C, and this process releases the greatest amount of exothermic heat

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition during thermal runaway elevates cell temperature more than 300°C within a few seconds.
claims.quote: This chemical reaction elevates the cell temperature more than 300 °C within a few seconds, resulting the maximum temperature because of the significant amount of exothermic heat during TR.

claims.claim: The NMC cell reached a higher maximum temperature of 488.4°C at approximately 1850 s during thermal runaway under oven test conditions, compared to the LFP cell which reached a maximum temperature of 345.7°C at approximately 3200 s.
claims.quote: temperature at the bottom side of the NMC cell reaches the maximum temperature of 488.4 °C at approximately 1850 s, corresponding to the chemical reaction of both positive electrode and electrolyte... Temperature for the bottom side of the LFP cell then exceeds that of the oven plate... finally reaches the maximum temperature of 345.7 °C at approximately 3200 s

claims.claim: Under CC discharge at 3C, the core temperature is 10°C higher than the surface temperature, indicating that fast charging protocol may induce significant thermal gradient in the cell.
claims.quote: the core temperature is 10 °C higher than the surface temperature under the CC discharge condition at 3C... This result demonstrates that fast charging protocol might induce significant thermal gradient in the cell.

claims.claim: As little as 12% of the energy released during thermal runaway is sufficient to trigger thermal runaway in adjacent batteries.
claims.quote: Feng et al. [8] explored the TR propagation mechanisms in LIB modules and found that as little as 12 % of the energy released during TR is sufficient to trigger the TR phenomenon in adjacent batteries.

claims.claim: Over 75% of the energy released during thermal runaway is used to heat the battery itself, with approximately 20% contributing to ejection, and less than 10% responsible for triggering thermal runaway in neighboring batteries.
claims.quote: Song et al. [9] investigated the energy distribution during TR in LIBs, revealing that over 75 % of the energy is used to heat the battery itself, with approximately 20 % contributing to ejection. Less than 10 % of the energy is responsible for triggering TR in neighboring batteries.

claims.claim: The severity of thermal runaway increases with state of charge (SOC).
claims.quote: Li et al. [11] and Sun et al. [12] independently studied the TR behavior of LIBs across different states of charge (SOC), both concluding that the severity of TR increases with SOC.

claims.claim: Increasing battery spacing and triggering temperature reduces the risk of thermal runaway propagation and alters the sequence of thermal runaway events.
claims.quote: Wang et al. [14] suggested that increasing the spacing and the triggering temperature of the batteries can reduce the risk of TR propagation in LIBs and alter the sequence of TR events.

claims.claim: In the deterministic trigger model, increasing battery trigger temperature corresponds to a gradual elongation in the time taken for complete thermal runaway of the module, indicating that propagation speed is reduced as trigger temperature increases.
claims.quote: in the DTM, an increase in battery trigger temperature corresponds to a gradual elongation in the time taken for complete TR of the module. It is indicated that the speed of TR propagation is reduced as the trigger temperature increases.

claims.claim: The proposed CNN-LSTM model achieved a maximum training accuracy of 0.967 and an F1-score of 85.02 for detecting abnormal heat generation in lithium-ion battery packs.
claims.quote: The performance of the proposed model has been proven to have a maximum accuracy of 0.967. The trained model performed an 85.02 F1-score, and all the risks can be detected timely.

claims.claim: When two TR cells are present, the detection model performs better than with a single TR cell because two TR cells release more heat to the flow domain, making the temperature contrast in the contour more prominent and easier to identify.
claims.quote: Using the same network indicates that the case with two TR cells is better than one with a single TR cell. This is because two TR cells can release more heat to the flow domain, and the contrast in the temperature contour will be more prominent, which is easy to identify by the model.

claims.claim: Using a larger time step of 16 s per sequence instead of 8 s per sequence reduced validation accuracy from 96.70% to 88.98%, because the larger time step obscures the distinction between 'risk' and 'safe' states and increases temperature change, negatively impacting prediction performance.
claims.quote: Experiment 1 outperformed Experiment 3, which shows smaller time steps achieved better accuracy. This may be due to the fact that the larger time step obscures the distinction between the 'risk' and 'safe' states, making it more challenging for the model to learn the differences. Additionally, a larger time step results in an increased temperature change, which may negatively impact the prediction performance of the proposed CNN-LSTM framework.

claims.claim: The introduction of abnormal heat production in a single cell (cell 6) increased the maximum temperature of the battery module by 12 K and amplified the temperature difference between cells.
claims.quote: The introduction of abnormal heat production increased the maximum temperature by 12 K and amplified the temperature difference.

claims.claim: The best CNN-LSTM model (Experiment 1: 8 s/sequence, 300×300 pixels) achieved an inference time of approximately 0.0904 s per batch of 8 frames, resulting in a detection delay of less than 0.1 s and a Recall of 100%.
claims.quote: the best model achieves an inference time of approximately 0.0904 s per batch (8 frames) at a resolution of 300*300, resulting in a detection delay of less than 0.1 s... Experiment 1 achieved a Recall of 100 %, indicating that the model successfully detected all actual risks throughout the whole TR process.

claims.claim: Under 1C charging conditions, thermal runaway occurs at 659 s, which is 33 s earlier than the 692 s observed under uncharged conditions.
claims.quote: under charging conditions, the thermal runaway occurs in 659 s, which is 33 s earlier than the 692 s under the uncharged condition

claims.claim: The thermal runaway advancement increases linearly as the charging and discharging rates grow, with this effect being more pronounced under discharge conditions.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway advancement linearly increases as the charging and discharging rates grow, and this phenomenon is even more apparent for the discharged condition

claims.claim: At 3C discharge rate, electrochemical heat production is 1528.89 J, which is much higher than the electrochemical heat produced at the same 3C charge rate.
claims.quote: The discharge process generates more electrochemical heat than the charge process, for example, 1528.89J at 3C discharge, is much higher than that at the same rate charge condition

claims.claim: Under 5C discharge conditions, the thermal runaway trigger time is 106 s shorter than under 1C discharge conditions.
claims.quote: The simulation results show that the thermal runaway trigger time under 5C discharge conditions is 106 s shorter than that of 1C

claims.claim: SEI layers generally decompose exothermically between 90–120 °C, and after SEI decomposition the cathode reacts with the electrolyte at trigger temperatures above 120 °C, while electrolyte decomposition occurs at temperatures above 200 °C.
claims.quote: SEI layers generally decompose exothermically between 90–120 ◦C [35]. After the SEI layer decomposes, the lithium ions of the cathode react with the electrolyte with a trigger temperature higher than 120◦C. The anode interacts with the electrolyte at even higher temperatures. Electrolytes decomposition at temperatures above 200 ◦C

claims.claim: Under CC-CV charging mode, the maximum temperature difference between internal and external battery temperatures at 0.5 C overcharge is 55°C, and at 7 C overcharge is 29.5°C.
claims.quote: Under the CC-CV charging mode, the temperature differences between the internal and external maximum temperatures at 0.5 C and 7 C overcharges are 55 ◦C and 29.5 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: Under CC charging mode, the maximum temperature difference between internal and external battery temperatures at 0.5 C overcharge reaches 75.2°C and at 7 C overcharge reaches 66.1°C.
claims.quote: Under the CC charging mode, the maximum temperature differences between the inside and outside of the battery at 0.5 C and 7 C overcharges reach 75.2 ◦C, respectively... C and 66.1

claims.claim: During CC overcharging at 5 C and 7 C, the battery pressure relief valve opens, with the maximum internal temperature reaching 115°C.
claims.quote: CC high-rate overcharging (5 C, 7 C) results in the activation of the battery's pressure relief valve, with the maximum internal temperature reaching 115 ◦C.

claims.claim: During CC overcharging, the pressure relief valve opens at 988 s for 5 C overcharge and at 686 s for 7 C overcharge, resulting in a sharp drop in internal temperature.
claims.quote: when the battery is overcharged at 5 C and 7 C for 988 s and 686 s respectively, the pressure relief valve opens, resulting in a sharp drop in internal temperature.

claims.claim: The voltage mutation threshold during CC overcharging increases with increasing overcharge rate, ranging from 5.0037 V at 0.5 C to 5.2610 V at 7 C.
claims.quote: during the 0.5 C overcharge period, the voltage mutated at 5.0037 V; during the 1 C overcharge period, the voltage abruptly changed at 5.0409 V; during the 3 C overcharge period, the voltage abruptly changed at 5.1587 V; during the 5 C overcharge period, the voltage abruptly changed at 5.2548 V; and during the 7 C overcharge period, the voltage abruptly changed at 5.2610 V.

claims.claim: When both parameters Poutlier and Pr>δ of the TR data are less than 0.05, the case is classified as sudden TR; otherwise, it is categorized as gradual TR.
claims.quote: when both parameters Poutlier and Pr>δ of the TR data are less than 0.05, the case is classified as sudden TR; otherwise, it is categorized as gradual TR.

claims.claim: The combined MOSE-AC and MMSE-LOF method achieves a 100% detection rate for gradual TR cases and an 86% detection rate for sudden TR cases.
claims.quote: when the two algorithms are combined, they achieve a 100 % detection rate for gradual TR cases and an 86 % detection rate for sudden TR cases

claims.claim: For gradual TR cases, the MOSE-AC algorithm triggered early warnings an average of 16,800 frames in advance, corresponding to approximately 6 days prior to TR.
claims.quote: For gradual TR cases, MOSE-AC was able to trigger early warnings an average of 16,800 frames in advance, corresponding to approximately 6 days prior to the TR.

claims.claim: For sudden TR cases, the MOSE-AC algorithm provided warnings an average of 9,400 frames ahead of actual TR onset, equivalent to roughly 3 days.
claims.quote: In sudden TR cases, the algorithm provided warnings an average of 9400 frames ahead of the actual TR onset, equivalent to roughly 3 days.

claims.claim: In gradual TR pre-failure data, the voltage extreme difference fluctuated between 0.1 V and 0.2 V, whereas in sudden TR pre-failure data, the voltage extreme difference fluctuated between 0 V and 0.02 V.
claims.quote: the voltage extreme difference of the E-bikes all had a fluctuation phenomenon of 0.1 V to 0.2 V... the voltage extreme difference of the E-bikes all fluctuates between 0 V and 0.02 V.

claims.claim: For 50 Ah LFP cells, immediate 1C discharge under forced convection at 105% SOC limits capacity degradation to 2.8% ± 0.3%, which is below the 3% industry failure criterion.
claims.quote: immediate 1C discharge at the reversible point limited the capacity degradation to 2.8 % ± 0.3 % (n = 3), which is significantly below the industry failure threshold of 3 %

claims.claim: Exceeding the 105% SOC reversible overcharge threshold results in irreversible capacity loss exceeding 7.5% ± 0.5%, accompanied by widespread rupture of the negative electrode SEI film.
claims.quote: samples charged beyond this limit experience a sharp capacity loss exceeding 7.5 %, with electrochemical analysis revealing widespread rupture of the negative electrode's solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film

claims.claim: Early thermal anomalies with a temperature rise rate exceeding 0.03°C/s were detected 1693 ± 45 s before thermal runaway onset, which is 1193 s earlier than voltage collapse detection.
claims.quote: The early thermal warning signal was detected 1693 ± 45 s before thermal runaway onset... Infrared thermal monitoring offers a crucial early warning advantage by detecting abnormal local temperature rise rates (>0.03 C/s) approximately 1693 s before thermal runaway—significantly earlier than voltage-based detection by 1193 s.

claims.claim: During normal charging (SOC < 100%), the battery surface exhibits lateral thermal stratification with a maximum temperature difference of 18°C, aligned with tab geometry due to current concentration effects.
claims.quote: below 100 % SOC, lateral thermal stratification (ΔT_max = 18 C) aligned with tab geometry... During normal charging, the battery surface exhibits a stable horizontal thermal stratification, characterized by significantly higher temperatures near the tab (lug) region compared to areas farther away. This pattern is attributed to the skin effect of current flow

claims.claim: At 105% SOC, the thermal field transitions from lateral stratification to a concentric pattern with a central hotspot, occurring 38 ± 5 s before mechanical deformation becomes visible to the naked eye.
claims.quote: at 105 % SOC, a transition to concentric thermal fields with central hotspots, emerging 38 ± 5 s prior to mechanical deformation... This reversal in thermal stratification occurs about 38 ± 5 s before the bulge becomes visible to the naked eye

claims.claim: Once battery SOC exceeds 120%, the temperature rise rate increases sharply to 0.62°C/s, and a 10% SOC increment drives surface temperature from 49°C to 86.1°C, compared to an average rate of 0.013°C/s between 90% and 120% SOC.
claims.quote: Between 90 % and 120 % SOC, the temperature rises slowly at an average rate of 0.013 C/s... However, once the battery exceeds 120 % SOC, the temperature rise rate increases sharply to 0.62 C/s, and a mere 10 % SOC increment drives the surface temperature from 49 C to 86.1 C

claims.claim: Thermal energy peaked at 37.9 kJ approximately 80 minutes into overcharging, followed by a 15% decline marking the transition from constant-current to constant-voltage charging, where cooling is identified as most effective.
claims.quote: Thermal energy peaked at 37.9 kJ approximately 80 min into overcharging, followed by a 15 % decline—marking the critical transition from constant-current to constant-voltage, where cooling is most effective

claims.claim: If the voltage increase rate exceeds 0.9 mV/s and no inflection point is observed, the battery can be definitively judged to have irreversibly transitioned into the thermal runaway pathway.
claims.quote: if the voltage increase rate exceeds 0.9 mV/s and no inflection point is observed, the system can be definitively judged to have irreversibly transitioned into the thermal runaway pathway

claims.claim: The multi-parameter early warning strategy combining temperature rise rate, spatial thermal uniformity, and voltage second derivative achieves a false positive rate of 4.7% and enhances warning lead time by 32% ± 5% compared to single voltage-based detection.
claims.quote: The multi-parameter warning strategy enhanced the lead time by 32 % ± 5 % compared to voltage-based detection alone... Multi-parameter warning false positive rate: 4.7 %

claims.claim: The reversible overcharge threshold of 105% SOC and associated thermal evolution laws are specific to fresh 50 Ah prismatic aluminum-shell LFP batteries at 25°C; universality for different capacities, packaging forms, states of health, or extreme temperatures requires further investigation.
claims.quote: the reversible overcharge threshold (105 % SOC) and the associated thermal evolution laws identified in this study are specific to fresh 50 Ah prismatic aluminum-shell LFP batteries under a standard temperature of 25 C. The universality of this threshold for cells with different capacities, packaging forms (e.g., pouch, cylindrical), states of health (SOH), or under extreme temperature conditions requires further investigation

claims.claim: During the irreversible overcharge trajectory, the temperature rise rate in the triangular region near the pressure relief valve increased significantly after the C state (105% SOC), while under reversible overcharge the negative electrode region exhibited a 28% enhancement in cooling due to endothermic lithium-ion reinsertion.
claims.quote: In the irreversible overcharge trajectory, the temperature rise rate in the triangular region near the pressure relief valve (measurement points 10–12) increased significantly after the C state, highlighting the intensive heat release associated with electrolyte decomposition. In contrast, under reversible overcharge, the negative electrode region (point 7) exhibited a 28 % enhancement in cooling, attributed to the endothermic effect of lithium-ion reinsertion following active discharge intervention.

claims.claim: Gas sensor false alarm rates can reach as high as 30% under complex operational environments due to cross-sensitivity to environmental conditions.
claims.quote: the cross-sensitivity of gas sensors to environmental conditions results in false alarm rates as high as 30 % under complex operational environments

claims.claim: The SEI film begins to decompose at approximately 90°C, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise.
claims.quote: As the temperature climbs to 90 ◦C, the SEI layer begins to disintegrate, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise.

claims.claim: When the temperature exceeds 130°C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, causing an internal short circuit through direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 130 ◦C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, and the interior of the battery is shorted by the direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: At temperatures up to 200°C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and interact with the adhesive, releasing toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: Up to 200 ◦C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and even interact with the adhesive, releasing a significant amount of toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases, such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: In NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively, indicating that gas signals provide earlier warning than temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.
claims.quote: Cui et al. [130] discovered that in NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively. The results indicate that the unique gas signal can not only provide an early warning during thermal runaway but also provide an earlier warning than the temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.

claims.claim: Using gas signal as the benchmark, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, compared to only 5.96 min for voltage signals and 0.5 min for temperature signals.
claims.quote: the figure indicates that when the gas signal is used as the benchmark, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, giving more time for escape and prevention, as opposed to when the voltage and temperature signals are used, which only allow for 5.96 min and 0.5 min of evacuation time, respectively.

claims.claim: The dynamic impedance in the 30–90 Hz band changes from negative to positive when the battery is overcharged, and taking 70 Hz impedance as an example, its warning time is 580 s earlier than the thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Lyu et al. [129] showed that the dynamic impedance in the 30–90 Hz band changes from negative to positive when the battery is overcharged. Taking 70 Hz impedance as an example, its warning time is 580 s earlier than the thermal runaway, successfully avoiding the thermal runaway accident caused by overcharging.

claims.claim: H2 is the first detected gas among six common gases (H2, CO, CO2, HCl, HF, and SO2) during LFP battery overcharging experiments, with detection time 639 s in advance of smoke and 769 s in advance of fire.
claims.quote: the results of the validation of the overcharging experiments of the LFP battery show that H2 is the first detected among six common gases, namely, H2, CO, CO2, HCl, HF, and SO2, and the detection time is 639 s in advance of the smoke and 769 s in advance of the fire.

claims.claim: The substable components of the SEI film start to break down at approximately 90°C, releasing gases including CO2 and O2 as well as combustible hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: The substable components of the SEI film start to break down at a temperature of approximately 90oC, releasing gases including CO2 and O2 as well as combustible hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: Most semiconductor gas sensors need to operate at temperatures above 200°C in practical applications, which is much higher than the operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries (−20°C to 60°C), making them difficult to encapsulate in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: most semiconductor gas sensors need to operate at temperatures above 200 ◦C to 60 ◦C in practical applications, and suffer from high operating temperatures, high power consumption, low stability, poor selectivity and complex synthesis processes. The operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries is from −20 ◦C to 60 ◦C, which is much lower than the operating temperature of metal-oxide semiconductor sensors, resulting in gas sensors that are difficult to encapsulate in lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: The model-based fault diagnosis algorithm for ISC detection successfully triggered the alarm 30 min before thermal runaway occurred in the case of RISC = 0.35 Ω ISC.
claims.quote: In the case of RISC = 0.35 Ω ISC, the algorithm successfully triggered the alarm 30 min before the thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: The PVDF binder reacts quickly with lithium metal or LiC6 at 350°C, producing fluoride gases and H2.
claims.quote: Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), a binder, for instance, reacts quickly with lithium metal or LiC6 at 350oC as shown below.

claims.claim: During discharge of a cylindrical 18650 LIB with an embedded FBG sensor, the temperature difference between the center temperature and the tank temperature is as high as 6°C, while during charging the temperature difference is 3°C.
claims.quote: During the discharge process, the temperature difference between the center temperature and the tank temperature is as high as 6 ◦C, while during the charging process, the temperature difference is 3 ◦C.

claims.claim: The surface temperature of a LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cell can reach 877°C when subjected to thermal abuse, and for a 3500 mAh cell, the total volume of gases released during deflagration was 5495 cm3.
claims.quote: They discovered that the surface temperature of the cell could reach 877oC when subjected to thermal abuse and that for a 3500 mAh cell, the total volume of gases released during deflagration was 5495 cm3, with the emitted gases including H2, CO2, CH4, CO, C2H4, and C2H6.

claims.claim: Traditional BMS technology can only provide early warning measures for obvious battery failures and cannot realize intelligent processing of data signals and prediction of unknown accidents.
claims.quote: traditional BMS technology can only provide early warning measures for obvious battery failures and cannot realize intelligent processing of data signals and prediction of unknown accidents.

claims.claim: Gas sensors have more early warning capability than sensors based on internal signature monitoring, but both lack predictive capability.
claims.quote: Gas sensors have more early warning capability than sensors based on internal signature monitoring, but both lack predictive capability.

claims.claim: Fire-impingement on LIBs resulted in temperatures in the immediate vicinity ranging between 200 °C and 900 °C, which aggravated thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: fire-impingement resulted in elevated temperatures in the immediate vicinity of the LIBs that were in the range of between 200 °C and 900 °C. Such temperature aggravated thermal runaway (TR) propagation, resulting in rapid temperature rise within the battery module and even explosions after 20 min of 'smoldering period'.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway and subsequent fire and explosion were attributed to the violent reduction of the cathode material coexisting with electrolyte when temperature exceeded 260 °C.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway and subsequent fire and explosion observed in the heating test was attributed to the violent reduction of the cathode material which coexisted with the electrolyte when the temperature exceeded 260 °C.

claims.claim: The self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT) calculated using the Semenov model was 126.1 °C and using the Frank-Kamenetskii model was 139.2 °C for the NCM/LTO battery.
claims.quote: The self-accelerating decomposition temperature of the cell, regarded as the critical temperature to trigger TR propagation, was calculated as 126.1 and 139.2 °C using the classical Semenov and Frank-Kamenetskii models.

claims.claim: NCM material in contact with electrolyte showed a sharp increase in heat flow from about 250 °C, reaching a peak of 1162.8 mW/g at 282.8 °C, with pressure increasing rapidly from zero to 3 bar above 270 °C.
claims.quote: a slow rise of the heat flow began from 150 °C and increased sharply from about 250 °C and reached the peak at 282.8 °C. The maximum heat flow was 1162.8 mW g−1. At temperature above 270 °C, following the generation of gases from the thermochemical reactions, the pressure increased rapidly from zero to 3 bar.

claims.claim: If the ambient temperature exceeds 126.1 °C or the shell temperature of the surrounding battery reaches 139.2 °C and the time exceeds the delay time of TR, thermal runaway would propagate from the failure cell to surrounding cells.
claims.quote: if the energy from the burnt battery is sufficient to make the ambient temperature over 126.1 °C or heat the surrounding batteries to 139.2 °C, and the time beyond the delay time of TR, TR would propagate from the failure cell to the surrounding cells although the thermal runaway progress of failure cell finished.

claims.claim: Increasing nickel content in cathode materials causes thermal decomposition to appear at a lower temperature and increases the amount of heat released.
claims.quote: With increasing the nickel content, the thermal decomposition of the cathode appears at a lower temperature, and the amount of heat released increases.

claims.claim: The decomposition of the s-SEI layer is more activated at high temperature than the decomposition of the p-SEI layer.
claims.quote: It has been examined that the decomposition of the s-SEI layer is more activated at a high temperature than the decomposition of the p- SEI layer.

claims.claim: Above 300°C, the binder reaction with lithium-ions is the main exothermic reaction at the anode, accompanied by H2 generation.
claims.quote: Previous studies reported that, above 300 ◦C, the binder reaction with lithium-ions is the main exothermic reaction at the anode with H2 generation.
