claims.claim: When the electrolyte is present at the anode, s-SEI regeneration (R13) is activated and results in more exothermic heat flow of s-SEI decomposition (R14) than in the absence of the electrolyte; however, depletion of lithium-ions by R13 reduces the exothermic peak of the binder reaction (R15).
claims.quote: The presence of the electrolyte at the anode activates s-SEI regeneration (R13) and results in more exothermic heat flow of s-SEI decomposition (R14) than in the absence of the electrolyte. In terms of the heat flow in the binder reaction (R15), R15 is less active because R13 depletes available lithium-ions.

claims.claim: The separator reaction (R7) occurs at a lower temperature when oxygen is present: it occurs at 320.09°C under 5% oxygen atmosphere, compared to 442.85°C without the cathode (argon atmosphere), and proceeds more quickly when oxygen content increases from 5% to 10%.
claims.quote: The separator reaction (R7) is generally more effective when oxygen is present in the system. As can be seen in Fig. S5, it can be confirmed that the separator is reacted at 320.09 ◦C under 5% oxygen atmosphere. Additionally, the reaction proceeds more quickly when the oxygen content in the Argon balance increase to 10% as compared to the 5% condition.

claims.claim: At 250°C isothermal condition, the pyrolysis reaction rate of EMC is 5.25×10⁻⁷/s, and it would take more than 7,000,000 s for all EMC to pyrolyze; for EC the rate is 4.94×10⁻¹⁴/s and it would take more than 300,000,000 s to fully pyrolyze, making pyrolysis inconsistent with the thermal runaway time scale of approximately 10 s.
claims.quote: At 250 ◦C, the pyrolysis reaction rates of EC and EMC are 4.94x10-14/s (compared to 2.10 × 10-2/s at 900 K) and 5.25x10-7/s (compared to 6.31x10-2/s at 700 K), respectively... At 250 ◦C isothermal condition, it takes more than 7,000,000 s for all EMC to pyrolyze, and more than 300,000,000 s for EC.

claims.claim: The combination of cathode and anode induces additional H2 and CO2 generation (R16) at around 250°C, attributed to the reaction of the anode with oxygen produced from the structural collapse of the cathode.
claims.quote: the combination of the cathode and the anode induces additional H2 and CO2 generation reaction (R16) at around 250 ◦C, and it is presumed to be the reaction of the anode with oxygen produced from the structural collapse of the cathode.

claims.claim: The rock-salt transformation reaction (R10) of the cathode occurs at a lower peak temperature when electrolyte is present compared to without electrolyte.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of R10 decreases compared to its peak in Fig. 3(a), implying the influence of the electrolyte on R10. This variation is confirmed more clearly using the deconvoluted peak of R10 in Fig. 3(e).

claims.claim: The reaction model achieves RMSE values of 1.91 mW for the electrolyte group, 21.79 mW for the cathode group, and 4.53 mW for the anode group when compared to DSC results.
claims.quote: The reaction model exhibits root-mean-square-error of 1.91 mW, 21.79 mW, and 4.53 mW in the electrolyte, the cathode and the anode, respectively, as compared to differential scanning calorimeter results.

claims.claim: Testing ten identical lithium cobalt oxide cells revealed a nearly twofold range in self-heating onset temperature (67.4°C to 123.8°C), a full order of magnitude variation in thermal runaway delay time (122 min to 1584 min), and a twofold range in maximum temperature (345.1°C to 726.7°C).
claims.quote: Zhang et al. [5] tested ten identical lithium cobalt oxide cells and found a nearly twofold range of variation in self-heating onset temperature (67.4 ◦C to 123.8 ◦C), a full order of magnitude variation in thermal runaway delay time (122 min to 1584 min), and a similar twofold range in the maximum temperature (345.1 ◦C to 726.7 ◦C).

claims.claim: Repeated component-scale DSC experiments showed heat release standard deviation values of up to 22% of the mean values, and the complete disappearance of certain peaks in repeated experiments.
claims.quote: Lenz et al. [11] repeated component-scale DSC experiments and found numerous sources of uncertainty in the data, from heat release standard deviation values of up to 22% of the mean values (indicating even wider confidence intervals) to the complete disappearance of certain peaks in repeated experiments.

claims.claim: DSC-informed kinetic uncertainty produces 2σ ranges on the initiation and maximum temperatures of 9–17% in ARC cell simulations.
claims.quote: DSC-informed kinetic uncertainty has a significant impact on ARC cell simulation results (for example, 2𝜎 ranges on the initiation and maximum temperatures of 9–17%)

claims.claim: 17% of KDE-sampled ARC simulation cases did not trigger internal short circuit, while 7% triggered significantly delayed internal short circuit, and 76% triggered uncertain but tightly clustered internal short circuit.
claims.quote: 17% of the KDE-sampled cases here did not trigger internal short circuit... 7% of the KDE-sampled cases here triggered internal short circuit late... the 76% of cases that are included in Fig. 6(d), i.e. the cases that triggered uncertain but tightly clustered internal short circuits.

claims.claim: Kinetic models inferred exclusively from DSC data cannot reproduce the low pre-exponential factor (A = 0.035 s⁻¹) required to bridge the gap between SEI decomposition and the onset of remaining exothermic reactions in ARC simulations, because typical DSC-inferred pre-exponential factors range from 10¹⁰ to 10²⁰.
claims.quote: Ref. [66] directly fits the kinetic parameters for this anode reaction to the experimental ARC data (rather than prior DSC data), resulting in a pre-exponential factor here of 𝐴 = 0.035𝑠−1 in the low-temperature range. This is a significant outlier, with typical ranges based on DSC data in the 1010 to 1020 range

claims.claim: The proportions of CO2, CO, and H2 in lithium-ion battery thermal runaway gases are 36.56%, 28.38%, and 22.27%, respectively.
claims.quote: the proportion of CO2, CO, and H2 in thermal runaway gases reaches 36.56 %, 28.38 %, and 22.27 % [6,7], respectively, which is considered as the primary characteristic gas in LIB thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Ti, Mn, and Mo dopants preferentially anchor to the S-surface of the pristine WSSe monolayer, with formation energies of -3.145, -1.282, and -3.776 eV respectively, compared to Se-surface formation energies of -2.022, -0.611, and -1.370 eV.
claims.quote: the doping of metal atoms on the S-surface of WSSe material could make the energy of the system more stable and facilitate the adsorption of gas molecules in the later work.

claims.claim: TM doping reduces the bandgap of WSSe from 2.041 eV to 0.295 eV (Ti), 0.795 eV (Mn), and 0.212 eV (Mo), with Mo–WSSe showing the largest reduction of 1.829 eV.
claims.quote: The band gaps of Ti–WSSe, Mn–WSSe and Mo–WSSe monolayers are 0.295, 0.795 and 0.212 eV, respectively. Compared with the pristine WSSe monolayers with a bandgap of 2.041 eV, that of TM-WSSe was reduced to some extent, and the Mo–WSSe was the smallest with a reduction of 1.829 eV.

claims.claim: The adsorption energy of TM-WSSe systems is nearly 7 times that of pristine WSSe, indicating that TM atom doping significantly enhances adsorption performance.
claims.quote: Among the remaining seven chemical adsorption systems, the adsorption energy are almost 7 times higher than intrinsic WSSe.

claims.claim: Recovery time exhibits a negative correlation with temperature, and higher adsorption energy causes a prolonged desorption process.
claims.quote: The recovery time exhibits a negative correlation with temperature, and higher adsorption energy causes a prolonged desorption process.

claims.claim: The electrochemical impedance of lithium-ion batteries first decreases and then increases with increasing temperature, with a change of 3–4 orders of magnitude before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the electrochemical impedance of LIBs changes by 3–4 orders of magnitude before TR

claims.claim: As temperature rises in a lithium-ion battery, micropore closure in the separator and electrolyte vaporization between electrodes lead to an increase in resistance R and capacitance C, causing impedance to increase.
claims.quote: as the temperature further rises, the micropores in the separator close, and vaporization of the electrolyte occurs between the electrodes, which leads to an increase in resistance R and capacitance C

claims.claim: The phase angle h of cell A at approximately 1 kHz has the lowest sensitivity to SOC, with a range of 1.97° and a variance of 0.39, while its fluctuation range across different temperatures is more than three times that of the SOC sensitivity analysis, and the variance is more than seventeen times higher.
claims.quote: the sensitivity of h to SOC is lowest around 1 kHz, with a range of 1.97° and a variance of 0.39. The fluctuation range of h in different temperatures is more than three times of that in the sensitivity analysis of SOC, and the variance is more than seventeen times

claims.claim: At a cell temperature of 45°C, the impedance drop value of cell A is approximately 2 mΩ, which equals half of the maximum impedance change range (|Z|max − |Z|min) at the specific SOC, and this threshold was selected as the first-stage thermal runaway warning indicator.
claims.quote: The temperature of the cell is 45 °C when the impedance drop value is approximately 2 mΩ, which is 1/2 of (|Z|max−|Z|min) at the specific SOC

claims.claim: In a parallel module, the voltage drop of an individual cell undergoing thermal runaway is not reflected in the module voltage, but the impedance of the failing cell increases abruptly, and this impedance increase can be used as a stage-III thermal runaway warning indicator.
claims.quote: The voltage drop of the individual cells will occur after the cell self-heating occurs, but due to the properties of the parallel module, the voltage drop will not be reflected in the module voltage. At this point, the impedance of the cell increased abruptly, in this case by 2.2 mΩ

claims.claim: In a parallel module experiment, the stage-I thermal runaway warning was triggered at a cell temperature of 50.1°C and the stage-II warning at 94.2°C, with advance warning times of 307 s and 181 s before thermal runaway, respectively.
claims.quote: the temperature of the cell was measured to be 50.1 °C... the temperature of the cell was 94.2 °C... Stage-I 307 [s]... Stage-II 181 [s]

claims.claim: Neglecting natural convection in the model resulted in a maximum peak temperature difference of -150 K and a maximum time-to-peak difference of 52.0 s compared to the full proposed model.
claims.quote: the maximum differences in the peak temperature between the proposed model and the model without convection were (cid:0) 150 K and 52.0 s, respectively

claims.claim: Neglecting thermal radiation in the model resulted in a maximum peak temperature difference of -52.4 K and a maximum time-to-peak difference of -125.0 s compared to the full proposed model.
claims.quote: the differences between the proposed model and the model without radiation were (cid:0) 52.4 K and (cid:0) 125.0 s, respectively

claims.claim: Increasing the cell-to-cell gap accelerated heat transfer but compromised energy density for the battery pack.
claims.quote: the presence of a cell-to-cell gap accelerated heat transfer but compromised energy density for the battery pack

claims.claim: When the cell-to-cell gap is 0 mm, natural convection does not change the sequence in which cells enter thermal runaway because there is no airflow.
claims.quote: natural convection did not change the sequence of the first and last cells entering thermal runaway. This may have been because there was no airflow, as the cell-to-cell gap was 0 mm in these simulations.

claims.claim: The larger the cell-to-cell gap, the larger the time interval until the last cell starts thermal runaway, due to increased heat dissipation facilitated by the broader gap.
claims.quote: the larger the gap, the larger the time interval until the last cell starts thermal runaway. This may be due to the increase in clearance and faster heat dissipation facilitated by the broader gap.

claims.claim: In a 20×20 battery pack with d=0 mm, α=90°, SOC=100%, and Tamb=25°C, approximately 86 seconds after the first thermal runaway reaction, all batteries in the pack were triggered.
claims.quote: Approximately 86 s after the occurrence of the ﬁrst runaway reaction, the TRs of all the batteries are triggered.

claims.claim: The ISC triggering condition in the model is set as when the jellyroll node temperature reaches the separator melting point of 165°C for the chosen NCA 18650 battery.
claims.quote: The ISC triggering condition is set as when the temperature of the jellyroll node reaches the separator melting point [39, 40] (165 °C for the chosen battery).

claims.claim: Under Mode I (low thermal contact resistance θc), local overheating on the battery surface causes the surface temperature to exceed the separator melting point quickly, producing a local short circuit that further accelerates thermal runaway triggering.
claims.quote: Mode I: low θc leads to a local overheating in the battery surface. The surface temperature exceeds the melting point of the separator quickly. The melting of the separator produces a local short circuit. This early-triggered ISC further accelerates TR triggering.

claims.claim: Under Mode II (high thermal contact resistance θc), conductive heat cannot concentrate on the battery surface, the surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time, and total TR propagation time is significantly longer than in Mode I.
claims.quote: Mode II: high θc prevents conductive heat from concentrating on the battery surface. The surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time. When the temperatures reach the TTR (melting point of the separator), the TR will inevitably be triggered. The total time of TR propagation is signiﬁcantly longer than that of Mode I.

claims.claim: The simulation indicates a threshold thermal contact resistance of θc = 0.89 K/W separating Mode I and Mode II thermal runaway propagation behavior.
claims.quote: The simulation indicates that the threshold θc = 0.89 K/W.

claims.claim: TR propagation speed increases with increasing ambient temperature and increases when battery spacing decreases, because smaller spacing reduces equivalent thermal resistance and accelerates heat transfer.
claims.quote: First, the TR propagation speed increases with Tamb, because a high Tamb corresponds to a high initial thermal energy within each battery. Second, the TR propagation speed increases when battery spacing d decreases. Decreasing d means decreasing the equivalent thermal resistance. The lower the thermal resistance, the quicker the heat transfer.

claims.claim: Battery packs with random heat transfer coefficients between 0 W/K and 2.7 W/K (average 1.35 W/K) showed TR propagation that was stable but slightly slower than packs with uniform thermal coefficients.
claims.quote: Simulation results show that the TR propagations of the battery packs with random thermal coeﬃcients are stable and little slower than the battery packs with even thermal coeﬃcients.

claims.claim: For a two-battery pack at d=0 mm (Mode I), the total TR propagation time Ttotal is approximately 3 seconds, whereas at d=0.01 mm (Mode II), Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 seconds.
claims.quote: Normally, the Ttotal of a two-battery pack at d = 0 mm is approximately 3 s (Fig. 6a, mode I). For another case at d = 0.01 mm, the Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 s (Fig. 6b, mode II).

claims.claim: For the pouch cell (Cell A), T3 detected inside the cell was 925.0 °C, T3 detected between two cells was 908.5 °C, and T3 at the cell surface was only 508.3 °C during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the pouch cell, the maximum temperature T3 detected inside the cell was similar to that detected between two cells, at 925.0 °C and 908.5 °C, respectively, versus only 508.3 °C at the cell surface.

claims.claim: For the prismatic cell (Cell B), T3 detected inside the cell was 959.0 °C, compared to 806.0 °C between two cells, and only 381.4 °C at the cell surface during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the prismatic cell, T3 detected inside the cell was 959.0 °C, compared to 806.0 °C between two cells, and only 381.4 °C at the cell surface.

claims.claim: For the cylindrical cell (Cell C), T3 detected inside the cell was 1222.2 °C, which is hundreds of degrees higher than the 600.4 °C detected at the cell surface during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the cylindrical cell, T3 detected inside the cell was 1222.2 °C, which is hundreds of degrees higher than that detected by the cell surface.

claims.claim: From onset temperature T1 to triggering temperature T2, surface temperature measurements are sufficient for investigating adiabatic side reactions because the heat generation rate is low enough to allow temperature equilibration between the cell interior and exterior.
claims.quote: Because the heat generation rate was low at this stage, the temperature could reach equilibrium, and therefore, the T1 values were the same whether they were detected inside or outside the cell.

claims.claim: After the triggering temperature T2, the internal temperature detection is the most accurate method for evaluating the maximum temperature and rate of temperature increase, because heat production increases faster near T2 and heat cannot transfer quickly from the cell interior to the surface.
claims.quote: However, heat production increases faster in the vicinity of T2, and a delay in the temperature responses can explain the phenomena observed in Fig. 9(a) and (b), where the internal temperature can be seen to reach T2 and increased sharply. However, the temperature detected between two cells increased tilt.

claims.claim: The maximum dT/dt detected by the internal thermocouple of the prismatic cell (Cell B) was 24.2 times higher than that detected between two cells, and 11.2 times higher than that measured at the cell surface during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum dT·dt−1 detected inside Cell B was 24.2 times higher than that detected between two cells, and 11.2 times higher than that measured at the cell surface.

claims.claim: The simulated internal temperature method (thermocouple between two identical cells) is an appropriate substitute for internal temperature detection only for the pouch cell, but is inaccurate for prismatic and cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: The placement of thermocouple between two identical cells is an appropriate substitute for the internal temperature detection method only for the pouch cell. For prismatic and cylindrical cells, the most accurate method is to directly measure the temperature inside the cell.

claims.claim: Energy release of the cell body during thermal runaway will be largely underestimated if evaluated from surface temperature measurements, with the internal measurement yielding approximately twice the energy of surface measurements for the pouch cell.
claims.quote: the energy calculated from the internal measurements was twice that calculated from surface measurements.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway behavior of a cell does not change significantly when capacity fade is less than 10% by different aging paths.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway behavior of cell does not have a significant change when the capacity fade is less than 10%, by different aging paths [24].

claims.claim: The cell with a higher nickel proportion in the cathode material always has less thermal stability, which is reflected by a lower T1 onset temperature.
claims.quote: The T1 value of Cell C was lower than that of Cell A and Cell B, led by the unstable of cathode material. The cell with more nickel proportion in the cathode material always has less thermal stability.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway T2 decreases as battery SoC increases, with the difference in T2 between 100% SoC and 25% SoC reaching 69.5°C.
claims.quote: The T1 of the tested batteries exhibited minor changes with SoC, while the T2 decreases as the battery SoC increased. The difference in T2 for the 100% SoC and 25% SoC batteries reaches 69.5°C, indicating that the exacerbation of the thermal stability of the battery at higher SoC.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature T3 during thermal runaway increases with SoC, reaching approximately 995.8°C at 100% SoC, which is 637.8°C higher than at 0% SoC (358.0°C).
claims.quote: For the battery at 100% SoC, the maximum temperature T3 is close to 1000°C, 637.8°C higher than that at 0% SoC, resulting in a more severe TR hazard.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rate during thermal runaway at 100% SoC (8640°C/min) is approximately 185 times greater than at 0% SoC (46.7°C/min).
claims.quote: The dT/dt max at 100% SoC is about 185 times greater than the dT/dt max at 0% SoC, leading to a huge difference in the T3.

claims.claim: Both activation energy Ea and pre-exponential factor A of the exothermic reactions in all three stages increase with SoC, with a significant rise observed when SoC increases from 50% to 90%.
claims.quote: Both Ea and A of the exothermic reaction of the three stages increase with SoC, as shown in Fig. 7. The kinetic parameters Ea and A rise significantly when the SoC increases from 50% to 90% SoC, indicating obvious deterioration of the thermal stability of the cathode and anode with the increase of SoC.

claims.claim: During Stage II of thermal runaway, the battery internal pressure increases sharply due to gas released by exothermic reactions, causing noticeable swelling, and when pressure reaches a threshold the bleed valve opens, causing a sudden decrease in temperature rate at the start of Stage III.
claims.quote: due to a large amount of gas released by the exothermic reaction, the battery's internal pressure increases sharply in Stage II, resulting in noticeable swelling of the tested battery. When the pressure accumulates to a threshold value, the bleed valve of the battery opens to eject a large amount of gas, resulting in a sudden decrease of battery temperature rate in the third stage (Stage III, from 181°C to T2).

claims.claim: At 20% SOC, thermal runaway did not propagate from cell 1 to cell 2; the maximum temperature cell 1 reached was 390.4 ± 7.4 °C at a TR onset temperature of 341.0 ± 7.8 °C, and the maximum temperature cell 2 reached was 252.6 ± 11.5 °C, which was below its TR onset temperature.
claims.quote: cells with 20 % SOC were tested in test No. 9 of Table 1, but the TR did not propagate between cells 1 and 2. The maximum temperature that cell 1 reached was 390.4 ± 7.4 ◦C when it underwent TR at 341.0 ± 7.8 ◦C, and the maximum temperature that cell 2 reached was 252.6 ± 11.5 ◦C which was lower than TR onset temperature of it.

claims.claim: Increasing SOC results in a decrease in TRPI until a minimum at 70% SOC (TRPI = 93.2 ± 3.8 s), after which TRPI increases again and peaks at 90% SOC.
claims.quote: it can be found that an increase in the SOC results in a decrease in the TRPI; at an SOC of 70 %, the TRPI is down to 93.2 ± 3.8 s. However, as the SOC further increases, the TRPI increases again and peaks at an SOC of 90 %.

claims.claim: The TRPI between cells 1 and 2 decreases as environment temperature increases, because higher environment temperature reduces convective heat loss from cell 1, shortening the time for cell 1 to reach TR onset temperature.
claims.quote: the TRPI between cells 1 and 2 declines as the environment temperature increases... the higher the environment temperature is, the smaller the convective heat transfer rate between cell 1 and the environment will be, the quicker cell 1 reaches the TR onset temperature. Therefore, the duration of the stage I will be shortened with the increase of the environment temperature

claims.claim: The TRPI between cells 1 and 2 increases as heating power increases; when heating power increased from 150 W to 350 W, the maximum temperature cell 2 could reach decreased from approximately 159.8 °C to approximately 100 °C.
claims.quote: the TRPI between cells 1 and 2 increases as the heating power increases... When the heating power is 150 W, the maximum temperature of cell 2 was about 159.8 ◦C, while when the heating power was 350 W, the maximum temperature that cell 2 could reach was only 100 ◦C.

claims.claim: The ANN model predicted that TR propagated within 37 s under conditions of 71% SOC, 80 °C environment temperature, and 100 W heating power, representing a more severe scenario than the RSM-predicted minimum TRPI of 61.08 s.
claims.quote: cell 2 ran into TR within 37 s after the TR of cell 1 occurred in the case of 71 % SOC, 80 ◦C environment temperature, and 100 W heating power, which brought significant challenges to the management of lithium-ion battery packs.

claims.claim: Among nine brand-new 18650 LCO cells tested at 100% SOC, self-heating onset temperature (Texo) ranged from 67.4°C to 123.8°C and thermal runaway delay time ranged from approximately 122 min to 1584 min.
claims.quote: self-heating starts to occur as low as 67.4 C and as high as 123.8 C, TR delay time also ranges from around 122 min to 1584 min.

claims.claim: For cells with similar self-heating onset temperature (~77°C), thermal runaway delay time still ranged from 802 min to 1584 min, indicating strong cell-to-cell variability independent of Texo measurement uncertainty.
claims.quote: If we look at the results of Batt 5–9, which all exhibit a similar Texo around 77 C, their TR delay time nevertheless ranges from 802 min to 1584 min, indicating a strong variation much larger than 5 C duration in different modes.

claims.claim: When reactant consumption during the heating period is accounted for, the thermal runaway delay time can be elongated by a factor of two under the higher end of self-heating onset temperatures.
claims.quote: With reactant consumption during heating further taken into account, the thermal runaway delay time can be elongated by a factor of two under the higher end of Texo.

claims.claim: For the one-step model, up to 25% of the reactant can be consumed before the system detects the exothermicity threshold when Texo is beyond 120°C.
claims.quote: For the one-step model, up to 25 % of the reactant can be consumed already before the system detects the exothermicity threshold, when Texo is beyond 120 C.

claims.claim: The upper and lower limits of the one-step kinetic model's predicted thermal runaway delay time span approximately one order of magnitude, and all experimental data fall within these bounds.
claims.quote: These two limits span by about one order of magnitude, indicating a large uncertainty for the thermal runaway delay time predicted by the one-step model. Through this comparison, it is intriguing that the experimental data are well within the upper and lower limits of the one-step kinetic modeling.
